evals.prompt: I'm using enums in TypeScript for user roles. Is this the right approach?
evals.expected_output: No runtime enum. TypeScript enums have runtime cost and can cause issues with tree-shaking. Use literal unions or as const instead: type Role = 'admin' | 'user' | 'guest' or const ROLES = { ADMIN: 'admin', USER: 'user' } as const; type Role = typeof ROLES[keyof typeof ROLES]. This is zero-runtime and type-safe.

evals.prompt: Write a type-safe event system where different event types have different payloads.
evals.expected_output: Use Discriminated Unions with a literal kind property: type AppEvent = { kind: 'user.created'; payload: { userId: string } } | { kind: 'order.placed'; payload: { orderId: string; total: number } }. Switch on discriminant for exhaustive handling. Add never exhaustiveness check in default case.

evals.prompt: How do I ensure a function parameter is always a string ID, not just any string?
evals.expected_output: Use Branded Types: type UserId = string & { __brand: 'UserId' }. Create a type guard function: function asUserId(id: string): UserId { return id as UserId; }. This prevents accidentally passing an email or name where a user ID is expected at compile time. Branded types add zero runtime overhead.

should_not_trigger: Write a Go HTTP handler for user authentication
